one_line_definition: A transducer whose radiating surface is a portion of a sphere, focusing ultrasound to a point near its center of curvature without auxiliary optics.
An air-coupled ultrasonic transducer that converts acoustic pressure to current via capacitance change between a biased conductive polymer film and a patterned backplate.
The condition where beam width at focus is determined solely by diffraction (wavelength and aperture), not aberrations or imperfections.
An exact integral formulation for the pressure field radiated from a planar source, used to predict the focused beam pattern of a piston transducer.

aliases_seen: full width at half maximum, -6 dB width
one_line_definition: The lateral width of an acoustic beam at the depth where intensity falls to half its peak value; standard measure of lateral resolution.
A diffractive optical-like device made of concentric annular rings that focuses acoustic waves through constructive interference; an external alternative to native transducer focusing.
The ratio difference between specific acoustic resistances of two media (e.g., solid and air) that limits sound transmission efficiency across an interface.
A nonlinear parabolic wave equation accounting for diffraction, thermoviscous attenuation, and nonlinearity for directional acoustic beams.
A method that expresses the source field as a sum of 25 Gaussian beams with complex coefficients, enabling closed-form solutions for propagating beams from a circular transducer.
The production of a wave at twice the fundamental frequency due to finite-amplitude (nonlinear) propagation; used for tissue characterization and NDE.
A material property quantifying the strength of quadratic nonlinearity in wave propagation; related to the ratio of second harmonic to fundamental amplitude squared.
Ultrasonic measurement configuration where a single transducer acts as both transmitter and receiver, reflecting sound off a target.
Ratio of reflected to incident pressure amplitude at an acoustic interface; +1 for rigid (hard) boundary, −1 for stress-free (pressure-release) boundary.
Perturbative approach to nonlinear acoustics in which the second harmonic is treated as a small perturbation driven by the squared fundamental field.
The axial distance ka²/2 at which the on-axis pressure of a circular piston transducer is maximum and beyond which the beam diverges; transition between near-field and far-field.
The ratio of focal length F to transducer diameter 2a; determines lateral beamwidth and depth of focus — larger FN yields wider beam but longer depth of focus.
The axial range around the focal point over which the beam intensity remains within a specified dB drop (e.g., 6 dB); proportional to λ·FN².
An exact solution to the wave equation with a radial Bessel function profile; theoretically nondiffracting (infinite depth of field) over an infinite aperture.
The radial wave number parameter in a Bessel beam (J_n(αr)) that controls lateral beam width; smaller α gives wider beam and longer depth of field.
A transducer composed of concentric ring-shaped elements that enables electronic focusing in all transverse directions symmetrically.
A family of limited diffraction beams formed by taking transverse spatial derivatives of a Bessel beam; exhibits angle-dependent sidelobes that can be made very low without reducing frame rate.
A technique that combines zeroth- and second-order Bessel beam scan-lines to reduce sidelobes by ~6 dB, at the cost of reducing frame rate to one-third.
The application of a non-uniform amplitude weighting across a transducer aperture to suppress sidelobes at the cost of widening the main lobe.
Method of computing pulsed transducer fields by decomposing the pulse into frequency components, computing each CW beam separately, and summing with appropriate spectral weights.
A laboratory material (e.g., gelatin + graphite particles) designed to replicate the acoustic properties of soft tissue, including frequency-dependent attenuation (~1.32 dB/cm/MHz).
The product of pulse spectral amplitude and hydrophone force-to-voltage transfer function; the practical quantity used to compute predicted hydrophone waveforms from transducer beam theory.
The mathematical relationship linking frequency-dependent acoustic attenuation to frequency-dependent phase velocity; enforces causality in attenuating media.
Secondary wavefronts originating from the rim of a piston transducer at the onset of transmission; arrive after the direct (plane) wave and can interfere with it.
A synthetic polymer with longitudinal bulk wave velocity ~2820 m/s and acoustic impedance ~3.3×10⁵ g/cm²/s; the preferred material for early ultrasonic lenses.
A plano-convex or plano-concave acoustic lens with one flat face (on the incident side) and one spherical face; achieves point focusing with ~100× concentration of sound power.
A plano-curved acoustic lens with one flat face and one cylindrical face; achieves line focusing with ~10× concentration of sound power.
